mechanism_narrative: Sustained month-over-month growth in nonfarm payrolls indicates a strengthening labor market and robust aggregate demand, leading to higher corporate earnings expectations and positive equity returns over the following quarter.
evidence_refs: fact:Post-2009 recovery was characterized by steady payroll growth and a bull market.
failure_condition: The rank information coefficient between 3-month changes in PAYEMS and 60-day forward S&P 500 returns is less than 0.03 over any 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: When year-over-year CPI inflation accelerates past a key threshold, it signals impending monetary tightening by the central bank, which increases discount rates for equities and negatively impacts valuations over the next month.
evidence_refs: fact:The high inflation of the 1970s and early 1980s corresponded with poor real equity returns.
failure_condition: S&P 500 20-day forward returns are positive in more than 55% of instances when the trigger condition is met.

mechanism_narrative: The simultaneous occurrence of a rising unemployment rate and accelerating inflation (stagflation) creates a difficult policy environment, eroding consumer confidence and corporate pricing power, leading to negative equity returns.
candidate_trigger: 3-month change in UNRATE > 0.2pp AND 12-month change in CPIAUCSL > 3.0%
evidence_refs: fact:The 1970s stagflationary period was characterized by poor equity performance.
failure_condition: The combined signal does not have a statistically significant negative correlation (p > 0.05) with 60-day forward returns during triggered periods across a full business cycle.

mechanism_narrative: In a low-interest-rate environment, an increase in the Fed Funds rate signals economic normalization and central bank confidence, which is positive for equities. The same signal in a high-rate environment would be negative.
evidence_refs: fact:Post-GFC recovery saw initial rate hikes (2015-2016) coincide with continued market gains.
failure_condition: In periods where FEDFUNDS < 2.5%, the correlation between positive FEDFUNDS changes and 20-day forward returns is negative over a 5-year period.

mechanism_narrative: A sharp, unexpected drop in retail sales growth indicates a rapid deterioration in consumer demand, negatively impacting forward-looking revenue estimates and causing an immediate negative market reaction.
evidence_refs: fact:Retail sales data releases are market-moving events.
failure_condition: The average 5-day S&P 500 return following a trigger event is non-negative.

mechanism_narrative: A sustained contraction in industrial production signals a slowdown in the manufacturing and goods-producing sectors, often preceding a broader economic downturn and negatively impacting equity markets.
evidence_refs: fact:INDPRO contracted sharply before the 2001 and 2008 recessions.
failure_condition: In more than 50% of triggered instances, the S&P 500 posts positive 60-day forward returns.

mechanism_narrative: A simultaneous decline in housing starts and an increase in the federal funds rate signals a collision between a weakening interest-rate sensitive sector and tightening monetary policy, creating recessionary pressure and negative equity returns.
candidate_trigger: Year-over-year change in HOUST < 0% AND 6-month change in FEDFUNDS > 0.5pp
evidence_refs: fact:This pattern was evident in the lead-up to the 2008 Global Financial Crisis.
failure_condition: The strategy of shorting the S&P 500 for 60 days upon this signal's trigger fails to produce a positive Sharpe ratio over a full business cycle.

mechanism_narrative: A low and stable unemployment rate reflects a tight labor market at or near full employment, which supports robust consumer spending and corporate earnings, leading to positive equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:The period from 2015-2019 saw low unemployment and strong equity market performance.
failure_condition: When UNRATE is below 5.5%, the average 20-day forward S&P 500 return is not statistically greater than the long-term average return (t-test p > 0.05).
